prompt:
Third time we've hand-rolled a status field with a pile of if/else — the document workflow, the order lifecycle, now the import jobs. I want a proper tiny state-machine library, fsm.ts, and I have opinions about the shape:

export class StateMachine and export class InvalidTransitionError (extends Error, carries .from and .event so callers can log them).

new StateMachine(config) where config is { initial, context?, states, onTransition? }. states maps a state name to { on?, onEnter?, onExit? }; on maps event name to either a target state string or { target, guard? }. Validate the config up front: unknown initial state or any transition target that isn't a declared state must throw right there in the constructor, not later at send time.

Runtime behavior the tests in test_fsm.ts pin down (run: `node --test test_fsm.ts`):
- machine.state is the current state name; machine.context is the context object; constructing the machine fires NO hooks
- send(event, payload?) transitions and returns the new state name; an event with no transition from the current state throws InvalidTransitionError
- guards get (context, payload); a guard returning false BLOCKS the transition quietly — state unchanged, send returns the current state, zero hooks fire
- hook order on a successful transition is strict: old state's onExit, then the machine-level onTransition, then the new state's onEnter; each hook receives one object { from, to, event, payload, context }
- an explicit self-transition re-fires exit and enter
- can(event, payload?) says whether send would succeed (guard-aware), without side effects
- hooks may mutate context and later guards must see it

No deps, node's test runner only, and the tests are the spec — don't edit them.
